EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A swell from a strong low far northwest of the Aloha State caused surf of 10 to 16 feet along the north-facing shores of Niihau, Kauai, Oahu, Molokai, and Maui; and 6 to 12 feet along the west-facing shores of Niihau and Kauai.  No serious property damage or injuries were reported.

A swell from a strong low far northwest of the Aloha State caused surf of 10 to 16 feet along the north-facing shores of Niihau, Kauai, Oahu, Molokai, and Maui; and 6 to 12 feet along the west-facing shores of Niihau and Kauai.  No serious property damage or injuries were reported.

Swells from powerful lows far northwest of the islands caused surf of 20 to 45 feet on north-facing shores of Niihau, Kauai, Oahu, Molokai, and Maui; 10 to 25 feet along the west-facing shores of Niihau, Kauai, Oahu, and Molokai; and 8 to 14 feet along the west-facing shores of the Big Island of Hawaii. The wrap-around effect was also in evidence as surf rose to 6 to 10 feet along the east-facing shores of Kauai, Oahu, and the Big Island. A professional surfer was injured at Waimea Beach Park on Oahu on December 7th because of the conditions. There were no reports of serious property damage, though the surf did deposit sand and other debris on coastal roads and property near the shore in several locales.

An upper trough moving over the islands from the northwest spawned heavy rain and flash flooding conditions.  A married couple from Hana, Maui, were apparently swept away by swollen stream waters on Thanksgiving night as they tried to make their way back to their home.  The couple's vehicle was found downstream the next day, but the individuals themselves were not located.  No serious property damage was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Hanalei Bridge was closed because flood waters inundated the structure and Kuhio Highway in that part of north Kauai.

Swells from powerful lows far northwest of the islands caused surf of 20 to 45 feet on north-facing shores of Niihau, Kauai, Oahu, Molokai, and Maui; 10 to 25 feet along the west-facing shores of Niihau, Kauai, Oahu, and Molokai; and 8 to 14 feet along the west-facing shores of the Big Island of Hawaii. The wrap-around effect was also in evidence as surf rose to 6 to 10 feet along the east-facing shores of Kauai, Oahu, and the Big Island. A professional surfer was injured at Waimea Beach Park on Oahu on December 7th because of the conditions. There were no reports of serious property damage, though the surf did deposit sand and other debris on coastal roads and property near the shore in several locales.

Weak and veering trade winds helped to sustain showers over windward sections of Oahu.  The rain caused small stream and drainage ditch flooding, and ponding on roadways.  There were no reports of serious property damage or injuries.

Trade winds died and convective weather developed across the island chain.  This resulted in heavy showers and thunderstorms forming over Oahu, the Big Island of Hawaii, and Maui.||Especially strong sea breeze conditions in southwest Oahu produced a tornado in the afternoon of February 11th.  One man suffered minor injuries at the Kapolei Golf Course, and there was damage to a roof and golf cart.  No damage estimate was available.

A strong high far north of the state produced sustained winds of 20 to 30 mph, and gusts as high as 50 mph, over all the islands.  In one instance, a woman in her 60s was hit by a falling branch on Oahu and critically injured.  She later died.  On the Big Island, a 100 foot by 40 foot section of the Kau High School roof was damaged.  Damage estimates were not available.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A woman in her 60s was walking along Waimanu Street in Honolulu when she was struck by a falling tree branch.  She was critically injured, and later died at the hospital.

A front moving through the state brought strong winds and heavy rain.  The system downed trees and power lines, knocking out power to some residents.  The winds also damaged roofs, carports, and, in one case, a heavy wooden greenhouse.  Most of the downed trees, power outages, and other weather-related problems occurred on Oahu on the 15th and early on the 16th.  The cost estimate for damages was not available.  There were no serious injuries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large carport roof was blown from its moorings, and a heavy wooden greenhouse was lifted off the ground and became perched on a neighbor's roof.  Each event occurred in Kaneohe on the island of Oahu.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dry conditions on the isle of Molokai contributed to a blaze that impacted parts of the island just north of Kaunakakai.  The fire blackened about 8000 acres of brush from the end of August through early September.  There were no reports of significant property damage or injuries.  Firefighters worked diligently to contain the stubborn blaze as it moved on several fronts toward the north and west, being sustained by the dry vegetation and variable winds.  The cause of the fire was under investigation.

A strong low north of the Aloha State generated surf of 15 to 25 feet along the north-facing shores, and 8 to 15 feet along the east-facing shores, of all islands except Lanai.  The especially high surf kept lifeguards busy helping people in distress.  One surfer died in waters off Anahola in Kauai during the afternoon of the 13th.  No significant property damage was reported from the high surf.||The low also destabilized the atmosphere over the islands and caused heavy showers and thunderstorms on Oahu and in Maui County.  There were no reports of serious property damage or injuries from the precipitation.

Dry conditions on the isle of Molokai contributed to a blaze that impacted parts of the island just north of Kaunakakai.  The fire blackened about 8000 acres of brush through the end of August, though it continued to burn into September.  There were no reports of significant property damage or injuries.  Firefighters worked diligently to contain the stubborn blaze as it moved on several fronts toward the north and west, being sustained by the dry vegetation and variable winds.

The mix of high humidity and afternoon heating generated heavy showers over portions of Oahu. The rainfall rate was particularly high and lasted long enough to cause flash flooding near Waikane. No significant property damage or injuries were reported, though a few homes along Waikane Stream sustained some damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy Rain produced flash flooding in Waikane and Waiahole Valleys on windward Oahu.  Police and Emergency managers closed Kamehameha Highway for 2 1/2 hours due to high and fast moving water over a 100 yard section of the roadway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A robust upper trough destabilized the atmosphere over the Aloha State, producing heavy showers and thunderstorms.  The system eventually caused flash flooding conditions over parts of Kauai and Oahu.  No significant injuries were reported.  As far as damages, a home on Oahu collapsed as a stream bank eroded.

Upper level instability, acting on residual low level moisture from an old frontal boundary, generated heavy showers, thunderstorms, high winds, and large hail over parts of Maui, Molokai, and the Big Island of Hawaii.  There were no reports of serious injuries or property damage.

Trade winds died and convective weather developed across the island chain.  This resulted in heavy showers and thunderstorms forming over Oahu, the Big Island of Hawaii, and Maui.||Especially strong sea breeze conditions in southwest Oahu produced a tornado in the afternoon of February 11th.  One man suffered minor injuries at the Kapolei Golf Course, and there was damage to a roof and golf cart.  No damage estimate was available.

Heavy showers and rain, generated by surface and upper troughs, impacted several islands.  The systems also caused flash flooding on two occasions.  No serious injuries or property damage were reported.
